symbols: arrows piercing the body — the instruments of martyrdom, endurance through suffering
red drapery — the blood of martyrdom and witness to faith
column or architectural support — the pillar to which Sebastian was bound
angels visible through window above — heavenly succor and the promise of glory for the faithful martyr
vine or ivy on the column — life persisting even amid death and suffering
description: A nearly nude male figure stands against a stone column, his muscular body pierced by several arrows. He is draped in a voluminous red cloth that hangs from one shoulder. His expression is one of anguished endurance, with wild dark hair and a strong physique. Behind him, a column supports an architectural element, with ivy or dead vines clinging to it. Through an opening above and behind, small angelic figures are visible in a bright sky over a distant landscape. The overall palette is dark and dramatic with the red cloth providing a vivid accent.
theological_description: Saint Sebastian, a Roman soldier and secret Christian, was condemned to death by Emperor Diocletian and shot with arrows. Grunewald's intensely physical depiction emphasizes the bodily reality of martyrdom. Though not a biblical figure, Sebastian's story embodies the New Testament call to faithfulness unto death: 'Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life' (Rev 2:10). The arrows recall the psalmist's cry 'Your arrows have sunk into me' (Ps 38:2), while the anguished endurance reflects Paul's testimony: 'I bear on my body the marks of Jesus' (Gal 6:17). The angels above represent the heavenly reward awaiting the martyr, as Jesus promised the dying thief 'today you will be with me in paradise' (Luke 23:43). Sebastian's story also parallels the Suffering Servant of Isaiah, who was 'pierced for our transgressions' (Isa 53:5). In Christian iconography, Sebastian became a patron against plague, his arrow wounds seen as analogous to the wounds of disease that God could heal.

scripture_refs.reason: Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the martyr's promise
Your arrows have sunk into me — the arrow-pierced body of the saint
I bear on my body the marks of Jesus — bodily suffering as witness
He was pierced for our transgressions — the pierced martyr as a type of Christ
